title: Symmetry-adapted qubit encoding with complete active space and Bravyi--Kitaev mapping for quantum chemistry on a quantum computer
abstract: We present a symmetry-adapted qubit encoding with complete active space (SAE-CAS) for quantum chemistry on fault-tolerant and near-term quantum processors. Building on exact-symmetry encodings, we extend symmetry-adapted mappings to approximate $Z$-symmetries corresponding to frozen-core and virtual orbitals, thereby reducing qubit requirements without significant loss of accuracy. We derive the mapping from the second-quantised Hamiltonian to active-space qubit Hamiltonians, prove its equivalence to the canonical CAS Hamiltonian with frozen-core and virtual-orbital projection, and integrate it with point-group and spin-parity symmetry encodings via affine Clifford transformations to maximise qubit reduction while preserving the target symmetry sector. The same framework also accommodates the Bravyi--Kitaev mapping, yielding an SAE-CAS-BK variant that is unitarily equivalent to SAE-CAS. Numerical benchmarking on nine small molecules using UCCSD and a hardware-efficient shifted-circular-alternating (HE-SCA) ansatz shows that SAE-CAS reduces qubit counts and Pauli-operator weight, yields shallower circuits with fewer parameters, and often accelerates VQE convergence; with HE-SCA it consistently reaches CAS reference energies in cases where JW-CAS does not converge within the tested budgets. We provide an open-source implementation in the Python package QuantumSymmetry. SAE-CAS offers a route to resource-efficient molecular simulations on fault-tolerant and near-term quantum processors.
summary: This paper introduces Symmetry-adapted qubit encoding (SAE-CAS) to make quantum chemistry simulations feasible on current quantum computers. By mathematically incorporating symmetry constraints from the Complete Active Space approximation, it drastically reduces the required number of qubits and circuit depth compared to standard methods. This advancement offers a crucial pathway toward resource-efficient molecular simulation.
main_problem: Developing resource-efficient methods for simulating quantum chemistry on near-term and fault-tolerant quantum computers.
main_result: The Symmetry-adapted qubit encoding (SAE-CAS) significantly reduces required qubits, Pauli operator weight, and circuit depth compared to standard Jordan-Wigner mappings, leading to faster convergence.
method: The authors derive a symmetry-adapted mapping that incorporates Complete Active Space (CAS) constraints as approximate Z-symmetries, allowing for qubit reduction via affine Clifford transformations.
model_or_system: The system is molecular electronic structure, modeled by the non-relativistic Born–Oppenheimer Hamiltonian in second quantization. The simulation uses UCCSD and HE-SCA ansätze on small molecules like H2O and O2.
key_observables: Qubit count, Pauli-operator weight, circuit depth/CNOT count, VQE convergence error relative to CAS reference energies.
important_parameters: CAS approximation parameters (frozen core, active, virtual orbitals); ansatz choice (UCCSD vs HE-SCA).
main_assumptions: The primary assumption is that treating CAS constraints as approximate Z-symmetries preserves the target symmetry sector while minimizing resource overhead.
figures_summary: Figures compare circuit complexity metrics and qubit resource metrics across different encodings, showing SAE-CAS consistently outperforms JW-based methods.
paper_structure: The paper builds mathematically by deriving Hamiltonian mappings from second quantization to active-space qubits (SAE-CAS), proves its equivalence to canonical CAS forms, benchmarks it against other mappings (JW, BK) using small molecules and ansätze, and concludes with resource efficiency comparisons.
why_interesting: This work directly addresses the computational bottleneck of simulating strongly correlated electron systems on quantum hardware by providing a systematic method to exploit molecular symmetries for resource reduction.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1: Circuit complexity metrics corresponding to Tables I–II: circuit depth (UCCSD/HE-SCA), CNOT counts (UCCSD/HE-SCA), VQE parameter counts (UCCSD/HE-SCA), and HE-SCA circuit layers. SAE-CAS ( ) yields shallower circuits, fewer entangling gates, and fewer parameters than JW ( ), JW-CAS ( ) and JW-CAS (SF) ( ).

FIG. 2: Qubit resource metrics corresponding to Tables I–II: number of qubits and Hamiltonian Pauli count across all molecules in Tables I–II. SAE-CAS ( ) reduces both relative to JW ( ), JW-CAS ( ) and JW-CAS (SF) ( ).

FIG. 3: Optimisation and accuracy metrics corresponding to Tables I–II. Panels: (a,b) VQE iteration counts for UCCSD and HE-SCA; (c,d) absolute VQE energy errors relative to the CAS energy calculated in PySCF in units of Hartree. Encoding symmetry at the mapping level with SAE-CAS ( ) reduces iteration counts with respect to JW-CAS ( ) and JW-CAS (SF) ( ) and allows us to attain CAS energies with HE-SCA in cases where the unreduced JW-CAS ansatz did not converge within the same budgets.

FIG. 4: Diagnostics for the HE-SCA convergence limitations on JW-CAS, side by side for O2 (6,5) (left column) and CO (6,5) (right column). Top row, (a, b): gradient variance Varθ[∂⟨H⟩/∂θ0] on the JW-CAS HE-SCA ansatz over 50 random θ samples, as a function of layer count L. In both molecules the variance drops about two orders of magnitude from L=1 to L=5 and then saturates near 10−3; there is no exponential decay, ruling out a classical barren plateau. Bottom row, (c, d): per-trial absolute convergence error |EVQE −EFCI| on a log scale, over 10 SLSQP runs from independent uniformly-random initialisations at the same depth L=10 for both JW-CAS and SAE-CAS.
